docs.title: The Triad of Law (The City Government)
docs.text:
1. The Fraternity of Order (Guvners)
    • Philosophy: They believe the multiverse is governed by absolute laws. If you learn the legal loopholes of reality, you can rewrite existence.
    • Headquarters: The High Courts (The City Court)
  2. The Harmonium (Hardheads)
    • Philosophy: Peace can only be achieved through absolute, forced uniformity. If everyone agrees to obey the law, conflict ceases to exist.
    • Headquarters: The City Barracks
  3. The Mercykillers (Jailers / The Red Death)
    • Philosophy: Mercy is a crime against justice. Every crime must face its exact, cold retribution without exception.
    • Headquarters: The Prison

docs.title: The Cosmic & Personal Growth Seekers
docs.text:
1. The Athar (Defiers)
    • Philosophy: Gods are frauds. They are just highly powerful extraplanar entities, not divine beings deserving of mortal worship.
    • Headquarters: The Shattered Temple
  2. The Mind's Eye (Seekers)
    • Note: This is a new faction formed by the merger of the classic Godsmen and Signers.
    • Philosophy: The multiverse is a forge meant to refine individuals. By testing yourself and shaping reality, any mortal can transcend to godhood.
    • Headquarters: The Great Foundry
  3. The Society of Sensation (Sensates)
    • Philosophy: The multiverse only exists if you experience it. Truth is found solely through sensory input, art, danger, and luxury.
    • Headquarters: The Civic Festhall
  4. The Transcendent Order (Ciphers)
    • Philosophy: Hesitation invites failure. True harmony with the multiverse means acting entirely on pure, unclouded instinct without overthinking.
    • Headquarters: The Great Gymnasium

docs.title: The Materialists & Individualists
docs.text:
1. The Fated (Takers)
    • Philosophy: Might makes right. If you have the power to take something and hold onto it, it belongs to you by natural right.
    • Headquarters: The Hall of Records (The Tax Collectors)

docs.title: The Nihilists & Destructive Powers
docs.text:
1. The Bleak Cabal (Bleakers)
    • Philosophy: The multiverse makes absolutely no sense, and life has zero inherent meaning. You must find your own small purpose by helping others cope with the madness.
    • Headquarters: The Gatehouse (The City Asylum)
  2. The Heralds of Dust (Dusters)
    • Note: Previously known simply as the Dustmen.
    • Philosophy: This life is a cruel illusion. We are all already dead, and the only goal is to achieve "True Death"—purging all emotional attachments to find absolute peace.
    • Headquarters: The Mortuary
  3. The Doomguard (Sinkers)
    • Philosophy: Entropy is the only absolute truth. Everything eventually decays, breaks down, and dies—and it is a beautiful thing to witness.
    • Headquarters: The Armory

docs.title: 5. The Chaos Vector
docs.text:
1. The Hands of Havoc (Wreakers)
    • Note: A new ascendant blend of the old Xaositects and the Revolutionary League.
    • Philosophy: Tyranny must be burned away. Real freedom only exists when oppressive structures, organizations, and laws are systematically dismantled by chaos.
    • Headquarters: Scattered warehouses throughout the slums of the Hive

docs.title: Mercykillers
docs.text: { width="200" } The Mercykillers, also known as the Red Death, were one of the fifteen sanctioned philosophical and political factions of the Outer Planes. Originally based in Sigil, and later the stronghold called the Tower of Law in the Astral Sea. The core tenet of this faction was that the entire multiverse would become better through justice, as justice would purge an individual's evil and make them better. Once everyone was cleansed, the multiverse would reach perfection.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
The faction once ran the prison in Sigil, acting as the wardens and every so often hunting down those already condemned but not yet imprisoned. They did not make laws nor pass judgement, only administered punishment without leniency.

During the tenure of Factol Alisohn Nilesia, a select few Mercykillers chosen personally by the Factol herself became Justiciars and would hunt down those already convicted but yet to be imprisoned through the darkest pits and farthest points of the multiverse. They devoted their lives to chasing down heinous criminals that had slipped through the fingers of the law.

docs.title: Base of Operations
docs.text: The Mercykillers were originally based in the Sigil Prison from where they hunted down criminals of all types and brought them to justice. After the Faction War in Sigil, the remaining Mercykillers re-established themselves on a floating rock in the Astral Sea and built the Tower of Law, a grim fortress of fifteen levels, that held prisons, torture chambers, barracks, treasuries, and more. From this fastness they would mount forays into the other planes by way of a permanent teleportation circle inscribed in the Vanguard Room. They would round up prisoners accused of whatever crimes they decide upon during the raid and drag them back to their fortress to face the Red Death's brand of justice.

docs.title: Possessions
docs.text: Mercykillers wore bladed armor and at least one pair of manacles. Often a mask or hood would obscure their faces, ensuring the face of justice was detached from its duty.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text: The faction was allied with the Harmonium and the Fraternity of Order, as the Harmonium patrolled and caught the criminals, the Fraternity of Order sentenced the criminals for their crimes, and the Mercykillers jailed the criminals and kept them imprisoned until their sentences were over. The Mercykillers saw the Sign of One, Society of Sensation, and Revolutionary League as enemies and often clashed with members of those factions, especially those of the Revolutionary League as they sought to destroy the other established factions, particularly those that held up the system of Sigil.

docs.title: History
docs.text: The Mercykillers came to be during the Great Upheaval as the Sodkillers and Sons of Mercy united in their pursuit of justice and created their Eight Tenets of Justice more commonly known as the Mercykiller Code. For 633 years, they were the wardens and jailers of Sigil until the Faction War saw all factions disbanded or exiled from Sigil. The Mercykillers tore themselves back into the two factions of Sodkillers and Sons of Mercy, with the Sons of Mercy keeping the job as wardens of the Sigil prison. Many of those Mercykillers that were not in Sigil during this time however joined neither, and instead became roaming groups. That was until a Mercykiller named Andrezhej established a new fortress in the Astral Sea, and built the Tower of Law. From here the Mercykillers acted more like their Justiciars of old, hunting down and dragging back those who they thought guilty of crimes before either incarcerating them at the tower or executing them for their crimes.

docs.title: Members
docs.text: Members joined simply by showing up on the enlistment days that were held once every fortnight. If the potential member had no outstanding issues with the law and was willing to listen to the Eight Tenets of Justice and swear upon each one, then they were henceforth considered a Mercykiller.

docs.title: Amulet of Health
docs.text:
Wondrous item, rare (requires attunement)

Your Constitution score is 19 while you wear this amulet. It has no effect on you if your Constitution is 19 or higher without it.

docs.title: Cloak of Displacement
docs.text:
Cloak of Displacement _Wondrous item, rare (requires attunement)

While you wear this cloak, it projects an illusion that makes you appear to be standing in a place near your actual location, causing any creature to have disadvantage on attack rolls against you. If you take damage, the property ceases to function until the start of your next turn. This property is suppressed while you are incapacitated, restrained, or otherwise unable to move.

docs.title: Dragonwing Bow
docs.text:
Weapon (any bow), Rare (Requires Attunement)

The limb tips of this magic bow are shaped like a dragon’s wings, and the weapon is infused with the essence of a chromatic, gem, or metallic dragon’s breath. When you hit with an attack roll using this magic bow, the target takes an extra 1d6 damage of the same type as the breath infused in the bow—acid, cold, fire, force, lightning, necrotic, poison, psychic, radiant, or thunder.

If you load no ammunition in the weapon, it produces its own, automatically creating one piece of magic ammunition when you pull back the string. The ammunition created by the bow vanishes the instant after it hits or misses a target.

docs.title: Mimir
docs.text:
Wondrous item, Rare (Requires Attunement)

This skull shaped device is filled with knowledge. The device weighs 5 pounds and is covered with subtle etchings of planar sigils. As a bonus action, you can toss the device into the air, whereupon it floats at a distance of 1d3 feet from you and you can access its properties. While the mimir is floating, a creature other than you can use an action to grasp or net the device, either by making a successful unarmed strike against AC 22 or a successful DC 22 Dexterity (Acrobatics) check. You can use a bonus action to seize and stow the device. The device has AC 22, 25 hit points, immunity to poison and psychic damage, and resistance to all other damage. It is considered to be an object that is worn while it is floating near you. Esoteric Knowledge. While the device is floating, you can use an action to cast Legend Lore from the device. The device speaks the revealed lore aloud. Once this property has been used, it can't be used again until the next dawn. Planar Knowledge. The device knows basic, useful information about the planes of existence. While the device is floating, it verbally answers questions you or anyone you designate poses to it about that topic. It knows the information about the planes in the Dungeon Master's Guide, as well as basic information about the gate-towns of the Outlands.

docs.title: Soul Coins
docs.text:
Wondrous item, uncommon

Soul coins are about 5 inches across and about an inch thick, minted from infernal iron. Each coin weighs one-third of a pound, and is inscribed with Infernal writing and a spell that magically binds a single soul to the coin. Because each soul coin has a unique soul trapped within it, each has a story. A creature might have been imprisoned as a result of defaulting on a deal, while another might be the victim of a night hag's curse.

Carrying Soul Coins. To hold a soul coin is to feel the soul bound within it — overcome with rage or fraught with despair.

An evil creature can carry as many soul coins as it wishes (up to its maximum weight allowance). A nonevil creature can carry a number of soul coins equal to or less than its Constitution modifier without penalty. A non-evil creature carrying a number of soul coins greater than its Constitution modifier has disadvantage on its attack rolls, ability checks, and saving throws.

Using a Soul Coin. A soul coin has 3 charges. A creature carrying the coin can use its action to expend 1 charge from a soul coin and use it to do one of the following:

  • Drain Life. You siphon away some of the soul's essence and gain 1d10 temporary hit points.
  • Query. You telepathically ask the soul a question and receive a brief telepathic response. which you can understand. The soul knows only what it knew in life, but it must answer you truthfully and to the best of its ability. The answer is no more than a sentence or two and might be cryptic.

Freeing a Soul. Casting a spell that removes a curse on a soul coin frees the soul trapped within it, as does expending all of the coin's charges. The coin itself rusts from within and is destroyed once the soul is released. A freed soul travels to the realm of the god it served or the outer plane most closely tied to its alignment (DM's choice). The souls of lawful evil creatures released from soul coins typically emerge from the River Styx as lemure devils.

A soul can also be freed by destroying the coin that contains it. A soul coin has AC 19, 1 hit point for each charge it has remaining, and immunity to all damage except that which is dealt by a hellfire weapon (BG:DA, page 223) or an infernal war machine's furnace (BG:DA, page 217).

Freeing a soul from a soul coin is considered a good act, even if the soul belongs to an evil creature.

Hellish Currency. Soul coins are a currency of the Nine Hells and are highly valued by devils. The coins are used among the infernal hierarchy to barter for favors, bribe the unwilling. and reward the faithful for services rendered.

Soul coins are created by Mammon and his greater devils on Minauros, the third layer of the Nine Hells, in a vast chamber where the captured souls of evil mortals are bound into the coins. These coins are then distributed throughout the Nine Hells to be used for goods and services, infernal deals, dark bargains, and bribes.

docs.title: Tan Bag of Tricks
docs.text:
Bag of Tricks Wondrous item, uncommon

This ordinary bag, made from gray, rust, or tan cloth, appears empty. Reaching inside the bag, however, reveals the presence of a small, fuzzy object. The bag weighs 1/2 pound. You can use an action to pull the fuzzy object from the bag and throw it up to 20 feet. When the object lands, it transforms into a creature you determine by rolling a d8 and consulting the table that corresponds to the bag's color. The creature vanishes at the next dawn or when it is reduced to 0 hit points. The creature is friendly to you and your companions, and it acts on your turn. You can use a bonus action to command how the creature moves and what action it takes on its next turn, or to give it general orders, such as to attack your enemies. In the absence of such orders, the creature acts in a fashion appropriate to its nature. Once three fuzzy objects have been pulled from the bag, the bag can't be used again until the next dawn.

Tan Bag of Tricks

d8 Creature 1 Jackal 2 Ape 3 Baboon 4 Axe beak 5 Black bear 6 Giant weasel 7 Giant hyena 8 Tiger

docs.title: The Decrees of the Lady (The Absolute Laws)
docs.text:
The Lady of Pain does not care about street crime, theft, or local brawls. She has only a few rules, communicated silently through her floating servants, the Dabus. If you break any of these, there is no trial, no defense barrister, and no prison cell—she will either slice you to ribbons with her shadow or banish you to a torturous, inescapable pocket dimension called the Mazes forever.

  • 1. No Divine Entities Allowed: Gods, deities, and greater powers are strictly forbidden from entering Sigil. Their avatars cannot cross the threshold, and they cannot warp portals to force their way in. Sigil is entirely neutral ground.

  • 2. No Worship of the Lady: It is a capital crime to worship the Lady of Pain as a goddess. She despises devotion. Any cults that pop up trying to pray to her or build her shrines are ruthlessly and bloodily wiped out by her blades.

  • 3. Do Not Harm the Dabus: The Dabus are the caretakers of Sigil, cutting back razorvine and maintaining the city's infrastructure. Harming, attacking, or interfering with a Dabus is treated as a direct attack on the Lady herself.

  • 4. Do Not Disrupt the Stability of the City: Any action that threatens the fundamental existence or structural stability of Sigil—such as attempting to permanently seal all the portals, breaking the city torus, or sparking a full-scale war between planar armies in the streets—draws her immediate, fatal gaze.

  • 5. The Faction Cap: The Lady decrees that there may be no more than 15 active factions operating within the city at any given time to keep the political balance from shattering into absolute chaos.

docs.title: The Mortal Laws
docs.text: Everything else is managed by the Triad of Law (The Harmonium, The Fraternity of Order, and The Mercykillers). These laws are passed by a majority vote of the faction representatives inside the Hall of Speakers. Because it is a city full of demons, angels, and cutthroats, the mortal legal system is intensely bureaucratic and heavily enforced.

docs.title: Crimes Against the City & State
docs.text:
• Treason / Sabotage: Attempting to manipulate the city’s portals, revealing secret gate codes to enemy planar armies, or trying to block the city’s trade routes. Punishment: Execution by the Wyrm (a lingering, brutal public death reserved for traitors).

  • Tax Evasion: Failing to pay the city’s flat 5% commerce tax on all business transactions or the monthly income tax (10% or 3 coppers, whichever is greater). Punishment: Seizure of assets, heavy fines, and execution by the blade for high-ranking officials.

  • Forgery of Official Documents: Faking a Guvner warrant, a courthouse manifest, or a high-up’s signature. Punishment: Immediate exile from Sigil.

  • Hampering Justice: Bribing city guards, hiding fugitives from the Mercykillers, or lying under a Zone of Truth in court. Punishment: Hard labor for up to two weeks and fines up to 200 gold pieces.

docs.title: Crimes of Aggression & Property
docs.text:
• Murder: Killing a citizen without a sanctioned legal warrant. Punishment: Hanging or permanent life imprisonment in the Prison. (Note: Brawling is technically tolerated in bars, but the moment a body drops, the Harmonium moves in).

  • Illegal Slavery: Pressing a citizen of Sigil into slavery or kidnapping folks from the streets. Punishment: Up to 10 years of hard labor in the slave pits or the Lower Planes. (Slavery itself is legal in Sigil if the paperwork is filed properly by Lower Planar merchants or if you are transporting slaves through the city on a legal planar manifest—but poaching citizens illegally is a fast track to the gallows).

  • Theft and Fencing: Robbing a merchant or selling stolen goods in the Market Ward. Punishment: A fine exactly equal to the total value of the stolen goods, paid directly to the city treasury (with 10% split among the three factions of Law).

  • Vandalism & Arson: Setting fire to structures or damaging municipal property. Punishment: Imprisonment up to one week, covering the exact cost of repairs, and an additional fine of up to 100 gold pieces.

docs.title: Plane Properties and Alignment
docs.text:
• Official Names: Carceri, The Tarterian Depths, or Tartarus.

  • Planar Alignment: Neutral Evil / Chaotic Evil.

  • Cosmic Classification: Outer Plane.

  • Core Concept: A planar prison. It is designed to prevent escape; portals function as one-way entrances, and leaving requires specific, rare keys or divine intervention.

docs.title: Structure and Layers
docs.text:
Carceri consists of six distinct layers. Each layer is a vast space containing an infinite number of self-contained, spherical planets (orbs) that drift near each other.

Layer Canonical Name Environmental Characteristics 1st Layer Orthrys A realm of vast swamps, quicksand, stagnant rivers, and volcanic mountains. 2nd Layer Cathrys A layer covered in dense jungles and grasslands where the plant life produces corrosive, flesh-eating acid. 3rd Layer Minethys A desert environment perpetually subject to abrasive sandstorms that erode stone and organic material. 4th Layer Colothys A landscape composed entirely of immense, vertical stone canyons, chasms, and mountain spires. 5th Layer Porphatys A shallow, sub-zero ocean of black water subjected to acidic snowfall. 6th Layer Agathys A single, infinite sphere of solid black ice streaked with frozen red matter. It is the coldest layer.

docs.title: Inhabitants and Factions
docs.text:
• The Demodands (Gehreleths): The native fiends of Carceri. They are divided into three distinct castes (Farastu, Kelubar, and Shator) and serve as the wardens of the plane, tasked with preventing prisoners from escaping.

  • The Titans: A pantheon of ancient, giant-statured deities imprisoned in the bogs of Orthrys after losing a war against the Olympian gods.

  • The Bastion of Last Hope: A hidden fortress located on the layer of Orthrys, which serves as a major base of operations for the Revolutionary League (Anarchists) faction.

  • The Tower of Khin-Oin: A 24-mile-high fortress shaped like a spinal column, located on the layer of Othrys, controlled by the Yugoloths.

  • Petitioners: The souls of the deceased who were untrustworthy, treacherous, or backstabbers in life. They retain their memories but suffer from extreme paranoia, preventing them from cooperating with one another to organize an escape.

docs.title: Core Concept and Alignment
docs.text:
• Classification: A Gate-Town located in the Ringlands of the Outlands.

  • Connected Plane: It holds the permanent portal to Carceri (specifically linking to its first layer, Orthrys).

  • Alignment: Neutral Evil / Chaotic Evil.

  • Core Function: It serves as a grim border town, prison, and dumping ground for exiles, refugees, and criminals from across the multiverse.

docs.title: Geography and Layout
docs.text:
• The Structure: The city is perfectly circular, surrounded by a massive wall.

  • The Districts: Inside the walls, the town is laid out in five concentric ringed districts (or "ring roads") that wrap around the central plaza.

  • The outermost ring contains common housing, stables, and taverns.

  • The inner rings contain workshops, upscale estates, and government administration.

  • The Gate: At the absolute center of the city sits Gate Square. The portal to Carceri stands here, appearing as a large, four-sided archway constructed entirely out of razorvine that radiates a deep red glow.

docs.title: Planar Mechanics and Environmental Hazards
docs.text:
• Razorvine: The city walls, buildings, and structures are heavily choked and overgrown with dangerous razorvine, a plant capable of slicing through thick leather armor.

  • The Weight Effect ("Ball and Chain"): Due to its proximity to Carceri, all creatures within the town suffer from a supernatural physical drag. Movement speeds for all individuals are mechanically reduced by 5 feet.

  • Dimensional Anchor: Teleportation magic and plane shifting do not function within Curst. Portals exiting the town are nearly non-existent; the only reliable planar exit is the gate directly into Carceri.

  • The Threat of Sliding: Because the population’s shared mindset heavily mirrors the betrayal and despair of Carceri, Curst is at constant risk of a "planar slide"—a phenomenon where the entire city could permanently shift out of the Outlands and merge into Carceri itself.

docs.title: Key Landmarks and Factions
docs.text:
• The Curst Prison: A massive, underground labyrinth of spikes, bars, and towers that runs directly beneath the entire settlement. It is guarded exclusively by the Wall Watch.

  • The Wall Watch: The permanent military force of the city. They man the gates, track all incoming names, and restrict citizens from leaving. They do not officially rule, but they maintain absolute authority over the city's perimeter.

  • The Burghermaster's Estate: The official mansion of the town's ruler. In current canon, the estate and the current administration are heavily subverted by Demodands (Gehreleths), the native fiends of Carceri.

  • The Town Dump: A massive junkyard spanning the valleys outside the city walls. Items randomly lost across the multiverse frequently materialize here. It is occupied by rust monsters, cranium rats, and a green hag named Dolores.

  • The Traitor's Gate Tavern: A notorious local establishment run by an unfriendly planar fighter named Tainted Barse.

  • Active Underground Factions: The town contains highly active hidden cells of both the classic Anarchists (Revolutionary League) and the Hands of Havoc. It also serves as a operational hub for Hezobol, a prominent wizard who coordinates slave trafficking between Curst and the Gehreleths of Carceri.

docs.title: 1. The Clerk’s Ward (The Bureaucratic Heart)
docs.text:
This is the domain of scholars, lawyers, scribes, and middle-class planar merchants. It is clean, heavily patrolled, and remarkably quiet compared to the rest of the city.

  • The Vibe: Elegant stone buildings, sprawling libraries, and quiet cafes. The bureaucracy of the multiverse is filed away here.

  • Key Landmarks: The Hall of Speakers (the city’s parliament where laws are debated) and the Civic Festhall (headquarters of the Society of Sensation, offering incredible sensory magic and performances).

  • Party Note: The Harmonium watch is dense here. An open brawl will get you tossed into a cell within minutes. Everything must look civilized.

docs.title: 2. The Lower Ward (The Industrial Forge)
docs.text:
The industrial powerhouse of the city, named "Lower" because it is choked with smoke and foul sulfur rising from portals that link directly to the Lower Planes (like the Nine Hells and the Abyss).

  • The Vibe: Clanging iron, massive ironworks, choking smog, and constant heat. It is a gritty, blue-collar district powered by magic and sweat.

  • Key Landmarks: The Great Foundry (run by the Believers of the Source, who forge the city's weapons and infrastructure) and the Armory (where the city’s defensive siege weapons are kept locked up tight).

  • Party Note: The visibility is terrible due to the smog, making it a fantastic place for setting up an ambush. Just watch out for industrial accidents or high-level elemental hazards.

docs.title: 3. The Lady’s Ward (The High-Class Citadel)
docs.text:
The wealthiest, safest, and most arrogant neighborhood in Sigil. It is home to the city’s aristocrats, rich planar elites, and the most powerful faction headquarters.

  • The Vibe: Towering palaces, opulence, marble pillars, and beautifully maintained plazas.

  • Key Landmarks: The City Court (run by the Guvners), the Barracks (headquarters of the Harmonium), and the ominous Prison (run by the Mercykillers).

  • Party Note: You need a good reason and some official paperwork to wander this ward looking rugged. The guards here will stop and question anyone who looks like a mercenary.

docs.title: 4. The Market Ward (The Trade Hub)
docs.text:
If it exists anywhere in the infinite Outer Planes, you can buy it, sell it, or trade it in the Market Ward. It is a loud, sprawling, multi-cultural marketplace that never sleeps.

  • The Vibe: A chaotic sensory overload of exotic creatures, strange languages, bizarre magical smells, and endless rows of merchant tents and permanent bazaars.

  • Key Landmarks: The Great Bazaar (a chaotic open-air market covering several square miles) and the Grand Bazaar Vaults where the most dangerous artifacts are guarded.

  • Party Note: You can melt into a crowd of hundreds of different planar races instantly. You need high perception to track anything here.

docs.title: 5. The Guildhall Ward (The Working Class District)
docs.text:
Sandwiched right next to the Market Ward, this is where the people who actually keep the markets supplied live and work. It is populated by cross-planar craftsmen, tradesmen, and apprentices.

  • The Vibe: Row houses, guild houses, bustling workshops, and neighborhood taverns. It is crowded but generally orderly, as the guilds enforce their own peace.

  • Key Landmarks: The various ancestral Guildhouses (The Blacksmiths, the Alchemists, the Portal-Mappers).

  • Party Note: The locals here protect their own. If you try to drag a guild member out of his workshop you might find an entire neighborhood of angry workers blocking your exit.

docs.title: 6. The Hive (The Slums & Underbelly)
docs.text:
The largest, most populated, and most terrifying ward in Sigil. The Hive is a massive, chaotic slum where the law completely ceases to exist.

  • The Vibe: Total squalor, crumbling tenement shacks, lawlessness, and desperate violence. It is home to the destitute, the diseased, tiefling gangs, and members of the Bleak Cabal or Dustmen.

  • Key Landmarks: The Mortuary (the massive fortress where the Dustmen process all the corpses of the city) and the Gatehouse (the asylum run by the Bleakers).

  • Party Note: This is an incredibly dangerous area. The Harmonium never patrols here. In the Hive, you can use whatever lethal force you want but if you get into trouble, absolutely no one is coming to save you.

docs.title: Sigil
docs.text:
The city of doors / portals Shaped like a donut with the city on the inside ring We ended up here and we started in the Marble District of the lower ward. We need to straight up to the opposite side of the city

Sigil: The City of Doors

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text: Sigil is a bustling, planar metropolis located at the center of the Multiverse, floating atop the impossibly tall Infinite Spire in the Outlands. Shaped like a massive, hollow torus (donut), the city's streets line the inside curve, meaning looking up reveals the buildings and sky of the opposite side of town. It has no natural sun, moon, or sky—only an ambient luminescent smog that brightens and dims over a 24-hour cycle.

docs.title: Key Features & Rules
docs.text:
• The Portals: Sigil's defining trait. Virtually anything shaped like an opening (a doorway, an arch, a picture frame, a sewer grate) can be a portal to anywhere in the multiverse. However, every portal requires a specific Portal Key to open—which can be an object, a specific action, a phrase, or even a thought.

• The Lady of Pain: The enigmatic, bladed ruler of Sigil. She does not govern daily life, but she enforces two absolute laws: No gods are allowed in Sigil, and no one may disrupt the peace of the city. Worshiping her is strictly forbidden and results in being flayed or banished to her inescapable Mazes.

• The Cage: A common nickname for the city. While you can travel anywhere via portals, you cannot leave by physical means (flying out of the ring just loops you back or drops you into the void), making it a gilded cage.

docs.title: The Six Wards
docs.text:
1. The Clerk’s Ward: The bureaucratic heart of the city, filled with beautiful architecture, government buildings, and libraries. Home to the Hall of Speakers.

  2. The Grand Bazaar / Market Ward: The financial hub where you can buy or sell goods from any plane in existence.

  3. The Guildhall Ward: The center for artisans, craftsmen, and traders learning their crafts.

  4. The Lady’s Ward: The wealthiest district, housing the city’s elite, high-end courts, and the Prison.

  5. The Lower Ward: An industrial, smog-choked slum dominated by foundries, portals to the Lower Planes, and the Shattered Temple.

  6. The Hive: The poorest, most dangerous, and chaotic ward. A sprawling slum where the desperate, the lawless, and the Heralds of Dust reside.

docs.title: The Ascendant Factions (The Core Philosophies)
docs.text:
Sigil is run by organizations tied to specific beliefs about the multiverse. Here are the 12 primary factions:

• Athar (Defiers): They believe the "gods" are just powerful frauds who don't deserve worship. They seek a true, higher divine force. (HQ: Shattered Temple)

• Bleak Cabal (Bleakers): They believe life and the multiverse have absolutely no inherent meaning. They cope by practicing selfless charity and running orphanages. (HQ: The Gatehouse)

• Doomguard (Sinkers): They celebrate inevitable decay, entropy, and the final destruction of all things. (HQ: The Armory)

• Fated (Takers): Radical social Darwinists. They believe those with power have the right to take whatever they want, and survival is entirely up to the individual. (HQ: Hall of Records)

• Fraternity of Order (Guvners): They believe that the multiverse is governed by absolute laws. By learning and exploiting these rules, they can control reality. (HQ: High Courts)

• Hands of Havoc (Wreakers): Chaotic anarchists dedicated to tearing down corrupt systems, laws, and societies to achieve total personal freedom. (HQ: Various warehouses)

• Harmonium (Hardheads): Authoritarian peacekeepers who believe universal harmony can only be achieved if everyone conforms to their strict ideals—by force if necessary. (HQ: City Barracks)

• Heralds of Dust (Dusters): They believe everyone is already dead, and this current life is just a cruel waiting room before achieving the ultimate peace of "True Death." (HQ: The Mortuary)

• Mercykillers (Jailers): Obsessively devoted to the absolute letter of the law and punishing the guilty. Justice must be executed without an ounce of mercy. (HQ: The Prison)

• Mind’s Eye (Seekers): They believe that each individual has a spark of the divine and can achieve literal godhood through personal growth, experience, and creation. (HQ: Great Foundry)

• Society of Sensation (Sensates): They believe the ultimate truth can only be found by experiencing everything the multiverse has to offer through the senses. (HQ: Civic Festhall)

• Transcendent Order (Ciphers): They strive to achieve perfect harmony with the multiverse by acting entirely on instinct and intuition, bypassing thought altogether. (HQ: Great Gymnasium)

docs.title: Things to Look For / Plot Hooks
docs.text:
• Faction Wars: The factions constantly vie for ideological and political control over the city's infrastructure.

• Accidental Portals: Characters might inadvertently trigger a portal just by walking through a doorway while holding a specific item (e.g., a copper coin or a dry leaf).

• The Dabus: The Lady of Pain’s silent, levitating servants who maintain the city. They communicate exclusively through floating visual rebuses (picture puzzles) above their heads.

docs.title: Catrina
docs.text:
Catrina is a figure that serves as companion and helper at the beginning of the path to the realm of the dead, not as executioners. However, they are capable of both, especially if misguided mortals try to prevent them from doing their duty, using their deadly but painless kisses to end a life in an instant.

The Catrinas always appear as a skeleton in festive clothing, usually in female attire, but sometimes in colorful male formal wear, and usually decorated with or carrying flowers. They are typically 5 to 6 feet tall and weigh 20 to 30 pounds.

docs.title: Chain Devil
docs.text:
[!statblocks] { width="200" }

Medium Fiend (Devil), Lawful Evil

HP AC Initiative 85 (10d8+40) 15 +5 (15) STR DEX CON INT WIS CHA 18 15 18 11 12 14 +4 +2 +4 +0 +1 +2 Mod +4 +2 +7 +0 +4 +2 Sav Speed 30ft Passive Perception (WIS) 11 Resistances Bludgeoning, Cold, Piercing, Slashing Immunities Fire, Poison, Poisoned Darkvision 120 ft (unimpeded by magical darkness) Languages Infernal, Telepathy 120ft CR 8 (XP 3.900, PB +3)

docs.title: Traits
docs.text: Diabolical Restoration. If the devil dies outside the Nine Hells, its body disappears in sulfurous smoke, and it gains a new body instantly, reviving with all its Hit Points somewhere in the Nine Hells. Magic Resistance. The devil has Advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

docs.title: Actions
docs.text: Multiattack. The devil makes two Chain attacks and uses Conjure Infernal Chain. Chain. Melee Attack Roll: +7, reach 10 ft. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) Slashing damage. If the target is a Large or smaller creature, it has the Grappled condition (escape DC 14) from one of two chains, and it has the Restrained condition until the grapple ends. Conjure Infernal Chain. The devil conjures a fiery chain to bind a creature. Dexterity Saving Throw: DC 15, one creature the devil can see within 60 feet. Failure: 9 (2d4 + 4) Fire damage, and the target has the Restrained condition until the end of the devil’s next turn, at which point the chain disappears. If the target is Large or smaller, the devil moves the target up to 30 feet straight toward itself. Success: The chain disappears.

docs.title: Reactions
docs.text: Unnerving Gaze. Trigger: A creature the devil can see starts its turn within 30 feet of the devil and can see the devil. Response—Wisdom Saving Throw: DC 15, the triggering creature. Failure: The target has the Frightened condition until the end of its turn. Success: The target is immune to this devil’s Unnerving Gaze for 24 hours.

docs.title: Dabus
docs.text:
Dabus were humanoids with white hair, goat-like horns, and skin of a yellow-tan or gray hue. They floated inches above the ground, their feet never touching the earth.

The dabus spoke only through visual rebuses, with illusory symbols that materialized and filled the air above their heads. They could understand all spoken languages, however.

docs.title: Deva
docs.text:
[!statblocks] { width="200" }

Medium Celestial (Angel), Lawful Good

HP AC Initiative 229 (27d8+108) 17 +4 (14) STR DEX CON INT WIS CHA 18 18 18 17 20 20 +4 +4 +4 +3 +5 +5 Mod +4 +4 +4 +3 +9 +9 Sav Skills Insight +9, Perception +9 Speed 30ft, Fly 90ft (hover) Passive Perception (WIS) 19 Resistances Radiant Immunities Charmed, Exhaustion, Frightened Darkvision 120 ft Languages All, Telepathy 120ft CR 10 (XP 5.900, PB +4)

docs.title: Traits
docs.text: Exalted Restoration. If the deva dies outside Mount Celestia, its body disappears, and it gains a new body instantly, reviving with all its Hit Points somewhere in Mount Celestia. Magic Resistance. The deva has Advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

docs.title: Actions
docs.text: Multiattack. The deva makes two Holy Mace attacks. Holy Mace. Melee Attack Roll: +8, reach 5 ft. Hit: 7 (1d6 + 4) Bludgeoning damage plus 18 (4d8) Radiant damage. Spellcasting. The deva casts one of the following spells, requiring no Material components and using Charisma as the spellcasting ability (spell save DC 17): - At Will: Detect Evil and Good, Shapechange (Beast or Humanoid form only, no Temporary Hit Points gained from the spell, and no Concentration or Temporary Hit Points required to maintain the spell) - 1/Day Each: Commune, Raise Dead

docs.title: Bonus Actions
docs.text: Divine Aid (2/Day). The deva casts Cure Wounds, Lesser Restoration, or Remove Curse, using the same spellcasting ability as Spellcasting.

docs.title: Gnolls
docs.text:

Medium Humanoid (Gnoll), Chaotic Evil

HP AC Initiative 22 (5d8) 15 (hide armor, shield) - STR DEX CON INT WIS CHA 14 12 11 6 10 7 +2 +1 +0 -2 +0 -2 Mod +2 +1 +0 -2 +0 -2 Sav Speed 30ft Passive Perception (WIS) 10 Resistances Immunities Darkvision 60 ft Languages Gnoll CR 1/2 (XP 100, PB +2)

docs.title: > Rampage. When the gnoll reduces a creature to 0 hit points with a melee attack on its turn, the gnoll can take a bonus action to move up to half its speed and make a bite attack.
Actions
docs.text: Bite. Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 4 (1d4 + 2) piercing damage. Spear. Melee or _Ranged Weapon Attack:__ +4 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. _Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage, or 6 (1d8 + 2) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack. Longbow. Ranged Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, range 150/600 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d8 + 1) piercing damage.

docs.title: Hag
docs.text: Hags were horrible witches of wicked intent and ancient origin, dark fey tied to primal forces whose foul magic and mysterious malevolence haunted fairy tales and nightmares. Equal parts hideous and heinous, hags embodied what it meant to be ugly inside and out, taking on the forms of unsightly old women. They were the antithesis of Feywild natives like the eladrin, for they were warped reflections of civilized beings that epitomized nature at its most repugnant.

docs.title: Campaign Notes
docs.text: Some sort of dead god that used to spawn portals // he's dead? and now he is causing portals to blink and the Mysterious Mage Female is somehow manipulating those portals

docs.title: Wiki Notes
docs.text: Aoskar, also known as the Power of Portals, the Keeper of Gateways, and the God of Doors was the self-appointed god of portals.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: His holy symbol resembled, and often took the form of, a large key.

docs.title: Divine Realm
docs.text: After being slain and cast out by the Lady of Pain, Aoskar remained adrift in the Astral Plane

docs.title: Worshipers
docs.text:
Aoskar was venerated as a god within his own temple in the City of Doors. At the peak of his influence, Aoskar boasted half the planewalkers in Sigil among his worshipers.Some even maintained the belief that the Lady of Pain herself, was merely an aspect of Aoskar.

Following Aoskar's death, a group known as the 'Will of the One'―a sect of the Sign of One faction in Sigil―took efforts to resurrect the dead god.

docs.title: Notable Worshipers
docs.text: • Fell, one of the dabus that served the Lady of Pain before joining the Sign of One.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
'Course, sages'll tell a body that the destroyed husks drifting in the Astral, like Aoskar, are neither dead nor alive, but linger somewhere in between.

— Editor's notes from The Factol's Manifesto.

After declaring his own divine influence over portals, Aoskar attempted to wrest control of Sigil from the Lady of Pain. While the exact specifics of this effort was not entirely clear, Aoskar developed a notable following in the city, enough to draw the Lady's ire.

Tired of his efforts to undermine her, the Lady slew the denizen of the City of Doors, and cast out his corpse into the Astral Plane. His temple was destroyed, his name stricken from all records in Sigil, and veneration of Aoskar was strictly forbidden in the Lady's city, under pain of death by the Mercykillers.

Aoskar's divine remains drifted alongside those of notable Faerûnian deities that remained dead for some time. Notable among these were: Amaunator, Bane, Bhaal, Ibrandul, Leira, Moander, and Myrkul.

The Athar faction—who claimed that true deities did not really exist, and whose existence was unfathomable by mortals―pointed to the dead gods of the Astral Plane, including Aoskar, as evidence of their beliefs.

The Four Doors portals ceased to work entirely as intended following Aoskar's death.

docs.title: Guvner Neverbloom
docs.text: Resides in Sigil as part of the Court District and the Fraternity of Order. There was a random monk who said she is not great to deal with and has no friends She is going to do the following - know who we are when we say a friend in the astral sent us - read our minds to tell who sent us - help us send the farmer home?

docs.title: Lady of Pain
docs.text:
runs Sigil, DM says we shouldn't cross her or her minions

While the Lady of Pain was by no means kind or compassionate, she was not necessarily cruel either. She seemed to care about little more than maintaining the status quo of her city. She had no interest in simple squabbles among her citizens or normal crimes such as burglary or murder. She only cared for things that might upset the balance or security of the city. { width="300" }

docs.title: Mysterious Mage Female
docs.text: She pulled us into her mage tower room where there was a loom with silver thread. Never gave us a name. She has a twin sister that controls automatons? She then sent us to Sigil to meet up with Guvner Neverbloom to see if we can turn off the portals? Not clear on what we are doing here

docs.title: Petra
docs.text:
A small male child sold into slavery who watched his other slave friends get eaten by gnolls dressed as clowns. I'm sure everything will be fine, that won't leave a lasting psychological mark or anything. We paired him up with Farmer Pait and arranged portal transport to Farmer Pait's home.

I think he's the odds on favorite for going murder hobo on Farmer Pait's homeland

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text: Artificers are masters of invention, magic, and mechanics. Unlike wizards who study pure theory or sorcerers who wield innate power, artificers view magic as a system to be engineered. They channel magical energy through everyday items, tools, and custom-built inventions—turning mundane objects into extraordinary gear.

docs.title: Key Mechanics & Features
docs.text:
Tools as Focus: Artificers must use a tool kit (like Thieves' Tools, Tinker's Tools, or Smith's Tools) as their spellcasting focus. Their spells are described not as chanting gestures, but as deploying gadgets, mixing volatile chemicals, or activating runes.

Magical Infusions: The class’s signature feature. Artificers can temporarily turn ordinary items into magical ones. They can imbue armor with extra defense, weapons with accuracy, or recreate existing magic items (like a Bag of Holding or Goggles of Night).

The Right Tool for the Job: They can magically manifest any artisan’s tool kit they need out of thin air during a short rest, ensuring they are always prepared to build or disarm.

docs.title: The Specialist Subclasses
docs.text:
An artificer's role changes dramatically based on their chosen field of study:

Alchemist: The mystic pharmacist. They mix volatile concussions, healing elixirs, and acid. They excel at support, healing, and utility.

Armorer: The magical engineer. They customize a suit of heavy plate armor into a high-tech exoskeleton. The armor can be configured for stealthy infiltration or heavy-tanking frontline defense.

Artillerist: The combat specialist. They construct magical eldrich cannons—which can walk on legs or be held like a wand—to blast enemies with fire, force, or protect allies with temporary hit points.

Battle Smith: The mechanical knight. They build a loyal, clockwork or homunculus beast called a Steel Defender to fight alongside them. They use their intelligence modifier for weapon attacks and act as protectors on the battlefield.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text: Barbarians are primal, ferocious warriors who rely on raw instinct, unmatched physical endurance, and unbridled fury rather than formal military training or heavy steel armor. On the battlefield, they are the ultimate frontline combatants, channeling their inner rage to shrug off lethal blows and deal devastating damage with heavy weapons.

docs.title: Key Mechanics & Features
docs.text:
Rage: The defining feature of the class. Entering a rage grants a barbarian resistance to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage, a bonus to melee damage, and advantage on Strength checks and saving throws.

Unarmored Defense: While not wearing armor, a barbarian adds both their Dexterity and Constitution modifiers to their Armor Class, allowing them to wade into battle bare-chested or clad only in simple furs.

Reckless Attack: A high-risk, high-reward tactical choice. A barbarian can choose to gain advantage on all melee weapon attacks during their turn, but in exchange, all incoming enemy attacks against them have advantage until their next turn.

docs.title: Primal Paths (Popular Subclasses)
docs.text:
A barbarian’s archetype defines the source and nature of their rage:

Path of the Berserker: The classic, unrelenting juggernaut. Their rage enters a state of frenzy, allowing them to make additional attacks as a bonus action, though it risks leaving them exhausted afterward.

Path of the Totem Warrior: A spiritually attuned warrior who bonds with animal spirits. Choosing the Bear totem grants them resistance to almost all damage types while raging, making them legendary tanks.

Path of the Zealot: A holy warrior fueled by divine fury. They deal extra radiant or necrotic damage during their rage and are uniquely easy for clerics to resurrect, virtually defying death itself.

Path of Wild Magic: A chaotic combatant touched by the Feywild or raw magical planes. Every time they enter a rage, a random magical effect triggers, such as teleporting across the battlefield or shooting arcs of force.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text: Clerics are the mortal representatives of the gods, acting as conduits for divine magic. Unlike wizards who memorize spells or warlocks who strike bargains, a cleric's power stems directly from their devotion and faith. They are versatile, armored spellcasters who excel at healing the wounded, buffing allies, and calling down holy (or unholy) wrath upon their enemies.

docs.title: Key Mechanics & Features
docs.text:
Divine Domain: Chosen at 1st level, a cleric’s domain represents their deity’s specific portfolio (e.g., Life, War, Trickery). It dictates their bonus spells, armor proficiencies, and heavily alters their playstyle.

Channel Divinity: A pool of divine energy that can be expended to fuel unique magical effects. All clerics can use this to Turn Undead (forcing undead creatures to flee), while their specific domain unlocks secondary, powerful abilities.

Prepared Spellcasting: Clerics have access to their entire spell list and can swap out their prepared spells every time they finish a long rest, making them incredibly adaptable to changing situations.

docs.title: Divine Domains (Popular Subclasses)
docs.text:
A cleric’s domain entirely shapes their role on the battlefield and in the party:

Life Domain: The ultimate healers. Their spells restore significantly more hit points than other classes, and they can heal multiple allies at once using their Channel Divinity.

War Domain: Holy knights who charge into the front lines. They gain proficiency with heavy armor and martial weapons, and can use their divine power to ensure their weapon strikes hit the mark.

Light Domain: Radiating holy fire. These clerics act more like offensive blasters, using spells like Fireball and Radiance of the Dawn to burn away darkness and impose disadvantage on enemy attacks.

Trickery Domain: Servants of mischievous or stealthy deities. They can grant allies advantage on stealth checks, create perfect illusory duplicates of themselves, and use misdirection to control the battlefield.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text: Rangers are elite wilderness survivalists, trackers, and scouts who bridge the gap between civilization and the untamed wilds. Equal parts warrior and nature-weaver, they use a blend of martial prowess, stealth, and primal magic to hunt down specific threats—whether those threats are marauding monsters, ancient dragons, or planar intruders.

docs.title: Key Mechanics & Features
docs.text:
Favored Enemy & Deft Explorer: Rangers are specialized hunters. They possess unparalleled knowledge of specific creature types and terrain, granting them bonuses to tracking, recalling lore, climbing, swimming, and navigating otherwise treacherous environments.

Primal Spellcasting: Drawing power from the natural world (similar to druids), rangers use magic to augment their combat and utility. Signature spells include Hunter's Mark (to track and deal extra damage to a target) and Pass Without a Trace (to shroud the party in near-perfect stealth).

Fighting Style: At 2nd level, rangers adopt a specialized combat style. While famous for Archery, they are equally deadly in close quarters utilizing Two-Weapon Fighting or defensive martial arts.

docs.title: Ranger Archetypes (Popular Subclasses)
docs.text:
A ranger's archetype reflects how they adapt to and master their chosen hunting grounds:

Hunter: The classic sentinel of the wilds. They focus on pure martial techniques designed to take down specific threats, specializing in either slaying massive giants or thin out overwhelming hordes of smaller foes.

Beast Master: A warrior who shares a deep, magical bond with a primal beast companion. The ranger and their beast (such as a hawk, wolf, or bear) act as a synchronized unit, moving and attacking together on the battlefield.

Gloom Stalker: Masters of the dark, often found in the Underdark or deep caverns. They are terrifyingly lethal during the first round of combat, possess darkvision that functions even against creatures with darkvision, and are entirely invisible while in pitch darkness.

Horizon Walker: Planar guardians who protect the material realm from extraplanar threats. They can sense nearby planar portals, magically teleport short distances between attacks, and convert their weapon damage into pure force energy.

Class: ArtificerClass

Subclass:

docs.title: Session 1
docs.text:
i come out of the woods and i fall into a portal that takes me to a room in a tower with Mysterious Mage Female. Hoping i don't lose my horse.

introduced to the ultra talkative Farmer Pait. We are in an astral plane.

Aoskar- god of portals - there's some sort of blink happening that spawn portals and teleports people. We were being teleported and the Mysterious Mage Female intercepted us and brought us to this round room , possibly in a mage tower, with a loom.

Shes sending us to Sigil - the city of portals run by the Lady of Pain

In Sigil we are to make our way to court district and find Guvner Neverbloom in the fraternity of order ( the area is home to judges, clerks, handling of legal affairs ). We are to say we have been sent from a friend in the astral to Guvner Neverbloom and they are going to read our minds to determine who sent us. We are warned to not fall beneath the cloak of the Lady of Pain, and to avoid her followers. We each get a rare magic item from Mysterious Mage Female. Listed in Players info

We are blinked out of the room to a ring world type city, its shaped like a donut with the city on the inside circle - this is Sigil. The center city of the Outlands

We arrive in a crypt with blood soaked floors and a bottomless pit. This is located in the lower ward of Sigil. The Lonkee steals some items out of caskets. We then find a secret door that opens to a temple? A Chain Devil and a Deva are inside playing dragonchess and conversing with each other with what seems to be extreme boredom, no fury or righteous indignation. The Chain Devil says something to us about putting the items we are stealing back. The Lonkee puts one back ( a maul ) and rolls sleight of hand to keep 2, a dragon wing short bow and a +2 scythe. We're going to sell the short bow. Corvis picked up the maul and could sense something from it. What did he sense?

We exit the temple onto a street.

We need to go up and across the donut city - currently in the marble district of the lower ward. We meet up with a monk debating with someone else. The monk wins the argument and the other person disappears. When we ask what happened he says the other person lost the argument and was probably transported to the maze ( i mention that she was transported there naked // Corvis grabs her clothes off the ground and stuffs into his bag ). The monk says we can hire a portal to take us all home @ 10k gold a person, or there are shady portal operators but they don't really have a 100% success rate. Tries to dissuade us from meeting Guvner Neverbloom.

The Reece pulls him aside for some additional info on factions and groups etc...

docs.title: Session 2
docs.text:
As we arrive in Session 2 we are in the same spot we were at the end of Session 1 the lower ward, a slum area full of quirky characters. We encounter a small, ramshackle carnival that has a high paced game of five finger fillet, we pass on taking a turn. We continue walking and come upon a nicer tent. There is a sexy red head in front of the tent who tries enticing all of us to spend some time with her. As she propositions each of us she shifts into what she thinks would be most enticing to us. For Corvis she turns into a Catrina. She's a changeling hooker. Lonkee gives her spellscar jerky, she eats the jerky and starts tripping balls. Says she sees something that isn't there.

We walk on and a little further on but still in the market area there are [[gnoll]] performers (clowns) and a small boy picking up coins; possibly soul coins. The gnolls are gnawing on bones, Corvis determines they are juvenille human or elven child bones. Its a group of 8 gnolls. We decide to attack as Corvis and myself are offended at the idea of eating slaves, especially child slaves. Reece walks into their tent that they are living in like he owns the place and I follow him, hoping to draw the gnolls in. They follow us.

The fight begins and Reece targets a group of 4 on our left. Corvis frontlines to the leader. I get to use the Dragonwing Shortbow for the first time and put out around 50+ damage in one turn. Lonkee attacks the group on the right. By our second round all the gnolls are dead or dying. We keep the leader alive and bring him over to a fire to question him. Who runs the slave trade? seems decentralized Who is your local contact? the fated, they have the biggest holdings and their tendrils are everywhere Who is your specific contact? Marcella with the fated. There is more debate where the gnoll tries to imply he bought the slaves fair and sqaure so he can eat them if he wants to. This actually seems true to some extent although it seems strictly forbidden to buy slaves as food ( you can murder / eat them if they misbehave, but you can't just eat them because you are hungry ). Corvis kicks him into the fire to die, then heals him to make him suffer more. We briefly loot and Corvis takes the bag of coins from the boy, sorts out the soul coins and puts them in a puch on his belt. 11 soul coins in total.

We are immediately confronted by a group of Mercykillers who say that we are being charged with Improper Assembly, Murder, Illegal Fires, Improper Disposal of Bodies, Disturbing the Public, etc... They are going to arrest us and take us to the court district. Corvis talks his way into letting us keep our weapons and no cuffs if we go willingly. We ask to be presented to Guvner Neverbloom.

I ask if the Mercykillers were looking for us specifically, but they weren't they just happened to be in the area.

We end up in prison row. Reece casts prestidigitation to clean us off and the Mercykillers don't like that and say we violated our agreement to go peacefully. They try to cuff us, but as a group we are all pretty much against that and willing to fight for it. We realize a fight is not ideal as we are in an area with multiple Mercykillers patrols. We are also right next to where Guvner Neverbloom should be working. There are guvner guards and we yell to them that we need to speak to Guvner Neverbloom. The Mercykillers try to stop us but we tell the guards they Mercykillers are unfit, did the crimes and are trying to pin it on us. The guards says "are these the ones getting kickback from Marcella?". The mercy killers arent happy about this but the guards are going to let us see Guvner Neverbloom. To add a little "fuck you" flavor I hand the head Mercykillers guard a business card and tell him to call me if he ever needs my help and walk past him to the Guvners court area.

One of the guveners guard proposes a debate vs the Mercykillers, the Mercykillers accepts and they start debating. The guvner guard eventually wins the debate and the mercy killer vanishes. Corvis casts zone of truth and asks one of the remaining Mercykillers if he took kickbacks from the slavers. he responds "i didn't".

The debate is over, the Mercykillers are walking away. We are on the steps of the courthouse. The Mercykillers prison is just across the square we are in. We are about to head inside and Lonkee eats a piece of spellscar jerky. She looks up and transports to the top of the court house where there is a Christmas feast waiting for her. She starts devoring the ham and throws some of the sides down for other people to eat. The guvner guards call for someone to bring her down from the top of the court house. An elysian knight ridiing a pegasus shows up to get Lonkee off the roof. The knight says to the guards something like "Don't ever call us for this kind of stuff again." and then flies away.

We find out the kids name is Petra, he's a boy, he was enslaved by the knolls and we rescued him, he's sticking with us and Farmer Pait is looking after him.

we are brought into a courtroom where there are 10 guards and 3 guvners, 1 of which is Guvner Neverbloom. They seem to be a group of 3 lawful judges - lawful good in the center, lawful evil to the right and lawful neutral to the left. Guvner Neverbloom is a lawful evil hag. The guards address the court and talk about the Mercykillers trying to arrest us but then losing a debate with the guards. Guvner Neverbloom smiles at this. Corvis bows to her and says "we were sent by a friend from the astral". When Corvis says that she stops smiling at us. She asks what does Mysterious Mage Female need. We explain about the Aoskar blink. Guvner Neverbloom says we should go to a bar and wait for her, but then asks why we were being escorted by mercykillers. We explain we were trying to save children from slavery / being eaten - but they seem ok with it and agree that we did something wrong. In the courtroom it seems you can't lie, you take dmg if you lie. There is some talk of favors and the Aoskar blink and also about corrupt Mercykillers taking bribes from slavers. We offer to help Guvner Neverbloom with the Mercykillers problem in exchange for sending Farmer Pait and Petra home. There's some more talk about how many favors this is. Guvner Neverbloom finally agrees but is reluctant to deputize us but i say i've been deputized so many times it would just be one more in a long list. They wait to see if I take damage but when I don't they agree to our plan.

The lawful neutral judge is a Catrina, in fact the same Catrina that the changeling hooker turned into. We have a short discussion with her and she offers us a Mimir to help us on our investigation.

docs.title: Over the table notes
docs.text:
• Do we want to go to Curst / Carceri ? DM keeps wanting to send us there.
  • We all level up to level 6
  • everyone gets a heroic inspiration
